coredata.dc:title: Prostate cancer biomarker citrate detection using triaminoguanidinium carbon dots, its applications in live cells and human urine samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Rajalakshmi, Kanagaraj
Deng, Tongtong
Muthusamy, Selvaraj
Xie, Meng
Xie, Jimin
Lee, Kang-Bong
Xu, Yuanguo
coredata.dc:description: Citrate is a tricarboxylate, plays vital role in prostate cancer (PC) and the level of citrate is an indicator for PC identification. Herein, triaminoguanidine carbon dots (TAG-CDs) prepared by one step hydrothermal method and used as a citrate receptor. Notably the TAG-CDs without alkaline treatment were highly fluorescent at pH 7 with high quantum yield (11.3%). TAG-CDs were characterized through TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis and spectrofluorimetry. It is noted that the average size was of 2.8 nm, the presence of highly disordered carbon, retain the functionality of TAG. The absorbance maxima obtained at 294 nm and good emitting response observed at 396 nm. The Y-aromaticity of receptor guanidinium moiety acts as Lewis acid and have peculiar interaction with Lewis base citrate via electrostatic interaction and also protons in the TAG participate hydrogen bonds with citrate, which causes quenching of TAG-CDs. From the obtained linear quenching equation the LOD was found to be 4 nM. The probe expressed high selectivity, high interference tolerance (500 – fold), fast response in 15 mins and good biocompatible. Finally, TAG-CDs utilized for the intracellular imaging of citrate in live MCF-7 cells, it showed good cytotoxicity and delivered contrast images in presence, absence of citrate. TAG-CDs detected the citrate level in human urine samples, the obtained results are validated with HPLC method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lewis acid-base sensor
Carbon dots
Triaminoguanidinium receptor
Prostate cancer biomarker
Fluorescent citrate sensor
Bioimaging in MCF-7 cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity test and confocal microscopy imaging
Sensing of citrate using TAG-CDs, its mechanism
Quenching mechanism, quenching and binding constants
Optimization of TAG-CDs towards detection of citrate
Cytotoxicity and bioimaging ability of TAG-CDs
Determination of citrate in urine samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Grant (No. 2019M661757) and Jiangsu postdoctoral research funding program (No. 2021K102B).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of TAG-CDs through TEM images with different magnifications.
Characterizations of TAG-CDs through (A) FT-IR spectra and (B) PXRD.

(A) UV– Vis spectra obtained for TAG-CDs (0.4 mg/ml) in the presence of each 0.1 µM addition of citrate to the probe from ‘a’ to ‘k’: 0 to 1 µM, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra obtained for TAG- CDs (0.2 mg/ml) in the presence of different concentrations of citrate (0–650 nM, each 50 nM). Inset: The linearity plot obtained between the fluorescence intensity against the concentration of citrate.

Bar chart representation of selectivity and interference ability of TAG-CDs with possible interferences, 1 to 19 are: citrate, benzoate, succinate, oxalate, acetate, sulfite, fumarate, glutamate, phosphate, malonate, nitrate, fluoride, lead, mercury, cadmium, urea, ammonia, creatinine and hippuric acid. For selectivity analysis 0.1 µM of each analyte was added separately to the probe solution, for interference analysis 0.05 mM of each analytes were added along with 0.1 µM of citrate.

(A) Bar chart representation of effect of pH on TAG-CDs in the absence (black) and presence (red) of 0.6 µM of citrate, (B) Plot of fluorescence emission intensity versus reaction time obtained for TAG-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of citrate (n = 4).
Confocal microscopy images obtained for (a) TAG-CDs stained cells and (b) TAG-CDs stained cells treated with 1 µM of citrate for 15 min.
Schematic representation of (a) synthetic procedure of TAG and (b) preparation and purification of TAG-CDs.
Schematic representation of interaction of citrate with TAG-CDs.
Comparison of LOD of citrate with earlier reports based on organic fluorophores and carbon dots.
Determination of citrate in urine samples and validation with HPLC method.

head.ce:title: Prostate cancer biomarker citrate detection using triaminoguanidinium carbon dots, its applications in live cells and human urine samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology, 217 Gajeong-ro Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34113, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Science and Technology, 217 Gajeong-ro Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34113, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citrate is a tricarboxylate, plays vital role in prostate cancer (PC) and the level of citrate is an indicator for PC identification. Herein, triaminoguanidine carbon dots (TAG-CDs) prepared by one step hydrothermal method and used as a citrate receptor. Notably the TAG-CDs without alkaline treatment were highly fluorescent at pH 7 with high quantum yield (11.3%). TAG-CDs were characterized through TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis and spectrofluorimetry. It is noted that the average size was of 2.8 nm, the presence of highly disordered carbon, retain the functionality of TAG. The absorbance maxima obtained at 294 nm and good emitting response observed at 396 nm. The Y-aromaticity of receptor guanidinium moiety acts as Lewis acid and have peculiar interaction with Lewis base citrate via electrostatic interaction and also protons in the TAG participate hydrogen bonds with citrate, which causes quenching of TAG-CDs. From the obtained linear quenching equation the LOD was found to be 4 nM. The probe expressed high selectivity, high interference tolerance (500 – fold), fast response in 15 mins and good biocompatible. Finally, TAG-CDs utilized for the intracellular imaging of citrate in live MCF-7 cells, it showed good cytotoxicity and delivered contrast images in presence, absence of citrate. TAG-CDs detected the citrate level in human urine samples, the obtained results are validated with HPLC method.

ce:section.ce:para: Citrate is a tricarboxylic anion, it involves a number of vital roles in biological processes. It plays a significant role in Krebs cycle, energy conversion and metabolism of cells. The abnormal levels of citrate in biological fluids are linked with various diseases [1–3]. Kidney dysfunction, nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis are the diseases related with the diminished level of citrate in urine [1–3]. Recently, citrate has been identified as an efficient prostate cancer biomarker due to its highly specialized anatomical function inside the prostate gland as well as the drastic change of its concentration level to the progression of prostate cancer [4–8]. Citrate levels are notably diminished in malignant prostate cells and deliver the onset progression of malignant. The concentration of citrate for healthy males is in the range of 50–200 mM, whereas in case of cancerous person is reported to be 2–20 mM [4–8]. Hence the detection of citrate in biological fluids and tissues are necessary to date. Several traditional analytical techniques are available for the detection of biomolecules such as electrochemical, gas chromatography, high performance liquid chromatography and capillary electrophoresis etc., [9–14]. However, these techniques have drawbacks such as expensive instrumentations, experts required for running the instruments, need of sample pretreatment, time and cost consuming methods.

Nowadays, fluorescent biosensors and imaging technology have been used extensively for the detection of small molecules because of its high sensitivity, cost effective, ease of operation, real-time spatial imaging, non-invasive detection, good selectivity and non-damaging detection of targets in living cells or tissues [15–29]. The most of the reported fluorescent citrate biosensors are mainly based on indicator displacement assays (IDA), in which the probe first forms complex with metal ion then this metal complex will detect the citrate [18–20,26,29]. The IDA type chemosensors have some drawbacks as, the probes can sense citrate if the binding affinity of metal and citrate should be higher than metal ligand binding affinity and this method shows low sensitivity, slow response, due to indirect detection of citrate. As a consequence, non-enzymatic, biocompatible, direct sensing of citrate will be benefitted. Recently, boronic ester was utilized for the direct citrate sensor based on Lewis acid base interaction type mechanistic strategy [7]. The compounds containing symmetrical diamides such as bispyridinium based amide and diketoprrrolopyrrole based diamides are used as the recognition receptor for the detection of citrate [21,24,25].

Owing the biocompatibility of guanidine, its derivatives have been studied extensively for detection of citrate and other anions in biological samples [30–33]. Generally, guanidinium moieties have peculiar interaction via two hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interaction with Y-shaped tri-anions rather than single anions [30–33]. A simple derivative of guanidine compounds are not fluorescent in nature and a large derivatives are not well suited for bioimaging reagent because of its solubility and biocompatibility compared with simple derivatives. On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs) prepared from simple guanidine derivatives are fluorescent as well as water soluble and most importantly biocompatible [11,22,34,35]. CDs are having zero dimensional with confined three dimensional excitons, low toxicity, chemical inertness, inexpensive raw materials, high photostability, simple synthesis, more stability towards pH and mainly high water solubility.

Based on the above studies, we introduced CDs from triaminoguanidine (TAG) as a citrate receptor. Herein, the simple citrate ions receptor TAG was synthesized successfully in a single step. The triaminoguanidine carbon dots (TAG-CDs) were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of TAG. The prepared TAG-CDs were characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, UV–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and spectrofluorimetry. From the TEM analysis the average particle size of the TAG-CDs is of 2.8 nm. The TAG-CDs expresses an absorbance band at 294 nm and reveals the emission peak at 396 nm with strong blue emission. After the introduction of citrate into TAG-CDs the emission intensity was decreased, based on the linear quenching sequence the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 4 nM. The main advantages of the present work is, the sensing strategy was carried out in physiological conditions, aqueous media with good biocompatibility, probe preparation is very simple and selective towards citrate specifically. The utility of the present probe was examined with live MCF-7 cell lines. The obtained survival ratio and confocal images revealed that our probe showed less cytotoxicity, good penetration into cells and responsive towards cellular citrate levels. Finally the probe TAG-CDs detected the citrate level in human urine samples, the obtained results are validated with HPLC method.

Guanidine hydrochloride, hydrazine monohydrate, 1,4-dioxane, citric acid and other usual chemicals and solvents were obtained from Aladdin, China. Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) medium 1640, fetal bovine serum (FBS), Hepes buffer solution, Dulbecco's phosphate buffered saline (DBS), streptomycin, penicillin and other cell culture materials were obtained from Hyclone (USA). From Beyotime, China the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased. Benzoate, succinate, oxalate, acetate, sulfite, fumarate, glutamate, phosphate, malonate, nitrate, fluoride, lead (Pb2+), aluminium (Al3+), mercury (Hg2+) and cadmium (Cd2+) and were obtained from Accustandard (J.K. Chemicals and Industries, China). All reagents were used as received without further purification. The double distilled water (DI water) was collected from the purification system from our laboratory.

Absorption spectra were measured by utilizing Cary 8454 spectrophotometer (Agilent Tech. USA) using polystyrene cells with a 1 mm path length. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by using a Cary Eclipse (Agilent Tech. USA). The Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer were used for Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra recordings. High Resolution-mass spectra were obtained using an HR-Mass-Synapt G2 instrument in positive mode to elucidate the mass of TAG. The pH of the solution was adjusted using a HI 2210 pH meter (Hanna Instruments, Woonsocket, RI, USA). For carried out cytotoxicity analysis, BioTek ELX800 microplate reader (Fisher Scientific, USA) was used. Fluorescence images were acquired using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP 5 II, Leica microsystems, USA) equipped with 63 × oil immersion objective lens using a diode laser as the light source. The TEM images were obtained from JEOL Jem advanced analytical HR-TEM, operating at 200 kV. The TAG-CDs dropped carbon coated copper grid was used for carrying out TEM images. The Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis was carried out by using Bruker AXS company, Germany, CuKa radiation (λ = 1.5406 nm) with a 2θ analysis range of 10-80° at a 5° min−1 scanning rate. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer) were carried out to determine the relevant functional groups.

Guanidine hydrochloride (300.00 mg, 3.14 mmol) was suspended in 10 ml 1,4-dioxane, hydrazine monohydrate (540 µl, 10.99 mmol) was added drop wise slowly under stirring and refluxed for 2 h. A white precipitate formed after cooling to room temperature. The detailed synthetic procedure was shown in Scheme 1 A. The product was collected by filtration and purified by washing with 1,4-dioxane to give a white solid compound TAG with an yield of 83%.
13C NMR (100 MHz, D2O-d 2) δ 159.5 (Fig. S1).
MS (m/z) calculated for CH9ClN6: 140.0577; found 141.5026 (Fig. S2).

The TAG-CDs were prepared via simple hydrothermal method as reported by previous article [36], for that the 0.5 g TAG was well dissolved in 20 ml of DI water and sonicated for 30 mins (Scheme 1B). The reaction mixture was transferred into Teflon lined autoclave reactor, treated with bubbling nitrogen for 1 h to remove the dissolved oxygen, create inert condition with in the reaction vessel and heated at 200 °C in autoclave. After 6 h the reaction mixture allowed to cool to room temperature, the yellow color solution were collected. The obtained TAG-CDs were further purified with centrifugation at 10,000 rpm about 20 mins to remove the large dots. Further the TAG-CDs purified by dialysis against DI water using dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut off = 1000) for 24 h to remove the excess of reactants and make it to a fine powder using freeze dryer. The obtained powder dissolved in DI water and used them for further characterizations such as PXRD, FT-IR, TEM, sensing (UV–vis, fluorescence) and bio-applications (cytotoxicity and bioimaging).

The probe solution for all the spectroscopy measurements was prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of TAG-CDs in the DI water. For UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy measurements, the concentration of TAG-CDs was kept at 0.4 mg/ml and 0.2 mg/ml, respectively. The other interfering solutions were prepared using double distilled water. For selectivity analysis each analyte (including citrate) were mixed with probe solution separately and measured the fluorescence behavior. For interference analysis, each analyte was added into the probe and citrate containing mixture and noted the emission spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity test and confocal microscopy imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
MCF-7 (breast cancer), Lewis cells (lung cancer) and MRC-9 (healthy lung tissue cells) cells were cultured with feeding of 10% FBS, 100 mg/ml penicillin and streptomycin in RPMI 1640 medium. Cells were grown and retained in an incubator under 5% CO2 environment at 37 °C. For cytotoxicity tests, the cells were cultured in a 96-well plate in culture media. The cultured cells were incubated with TAG-CDs at different concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 7 and 10 mg in 10 ml of DI water) for 24 h and 0.5 mg/ml MTT was added to the cells, incubated about 4 h. The produced formazan crystals were dispersed in 0.1 ml DMSO and read with a BioTek ELX800 microplate reader. The survival ratio (cytotoxicity) of the probe was calculated using the following formula, as reported earlier [37].
Cell
s u r v i v a l
r a t i o
( % )
=
Absorbance
o f
s a m p l e
Absorbance
o f
c o n t r o l
× 100

For taking live cell images, the cells were seeded in 35-mm culture dishes with media. The cultured MCF-7 cells were stained with a 0.2 mg/ml probe for 30 min and washed with DBS. The probe stained cells were incubated with 1 μM of citrate for 15 min. The confocal laser scanning microscopy (Leica TCS SP 5 II, Leica microsystems, USA) with diode laser as the light source and 63 × oil immersion objective lens were used to capture the fluorescence images.

The LOD was calculated based on the fluorescence intensities of the emission curves obtained from the fluorescence titrations. The standard deviation of the blank was determined by measuring the emission intensity of the probe about 15 times without analyte molecule. Also the emission intensity was noticed in presence of each concentration of analyte with three replicates and took the average intensity value. The average value of the emission intensity was plotted against the concentration of analyte. The LOD was calculated based on the slope obtained from the linearity plot showing as below:
LO D =
3
σ b
m
where ‘σb’ is the standard deviation of the blank experiments, and ‘m’ is the slope value of the linearity plot.

The TAG was chosen as the sole precursor for the preparation of TAG-CDs, since, it has rich nitrogen source and positive center. The positive charge carbon in the center of the TAG achieves the selective detection of negatively charged citrate ion via of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. The synthesis of TAG, which is the sole precursor and the source for TAG-CDs was prepared as per the procedure explained in the experimental Section 2.3. Using TAG the one step hydrothermal method was applied for the synthesis of TAG-CDs (experimental Section 2.4). The synthesis procedure was optimized by measuring the optical properties of prepared CDs at various reaction conditions such as different reaction time and hydrothermal temperature. The solution of 0.5 g of TAG dissolved in 20 ml of DI water was used for the synthesis of TAG-CDs by hydrothermal method. Initially, the absorbance, its corresponding excitation and the emission behavior was checked (Fig. S3A-C). The as prepared TAG-CDs were shown a characteristic UV–vis absorbance peak at 294 nm (Fig. S3A). The excitation and its corresponding emission nature were analyzed and depicted in Fig. S3B. It seems that the emission falls at 396 nm with its corresponding excitation of 303 nm. To obtain the finest excitation wavelength, the divergence ranges of excitation were employed from 280 to 310 nm and observed the emission spectra. Fig. S3C shows the emission spectra for TAG-CDs obtained at different excitations from 280 to 310 nm. The emission peak was observed at 372 nm while exciting at 280 nm, further increasing the excitation wavelength by each 10 nm the emission peak gradually redshifted along with increasing fluorescence intensity up to 300 nm excitation (Fig. S3C). Whereas in the case of 310 nm excitation, the emission peak intensity decreased in addition to gradual redshift, this phenomenon typically happened in CDs [38]. From these studies, the excitation wavelength of 303 nm was chosen for further analysis. Keeping this approximated spectral properties, we optimized the synthesis conditions to obtain the good emitting TAG-CDs. The reaction conditions were varied in terms of reaction time, hydrothermal temperature and investigated the optical properties of the obtained TAG-CDs (Fig. S3D, E). From the Fig. S3D-E, it seems that both hydrothermal reaction conditions the temperature and time affected the optical properties of TAG -CDs. Similar trend was observed in previous reports [36]. Based on the obtained results, the reaction temperature of 200 °C for 6 h is an identified as ideal condition for obtaining an excellent TAG -CDs.

The prepared TAG-CDs at optimum reaction conditions were utilized for further characterizations and sensing performances. The morphology of the prepared TAG-CDs were examined through TEM images. The TEM images show that the prepared TAG -CDs are having the particle size in the ranges of 2.4 to 4.8 nm with an average particle size of 2.8 nm, which are well dispersed without any form of aggregations (Fig. 1 ). Further, the functional groups present on the TAG-CDs, its stretching and bending modes were analyzed by FT-IR (Fig. 2 A). The characteristic peak for aliphatic primary and secondary amine NH stretching vibration band appeared at 3321, 3204 cm−1, the NH bending vibration falls at 1617 cm−1 [39,40]. The specific peaks for CN stretching and open chain imine CN was obtained at 1326 and 1681 cm−1, respectively [40,41]. From the obtained results for TAG and TAG-CDs Fig. 2A, the prepared TAG-CDs successfully functionalized or analogous to TAG. The obtained powdered XRD patterns were depicted in Fig. 2B. The PXRD pattern shows a broad band around 24.4°, which indicates that the presence of highly disordered carbon atoms, which is the characteristic PXRD pattern of CDs. The quantum yield of the TAG-CDs was calculated to be 11.3% by using rhodamine 101 as standard [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of citrate using TAG-CDs, its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing strategy falls under the category of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between the triaminoguanidinium moieties present in the TAG-CDs with the tri anion citrate [30–32]. Since guanidinium cation is favored the Y-aromaticity of the CN3 system, which considers as driving force for the deprotonation of guanidine scaffolds and becoming a Lewis acid character. It is well known that guanidinium ions (Lewis acid) could interact with tri anions such as citrate (Lewis base). The Lewis acid-base complex generation is achieved through electrostatic interaction between the central carbon atom of CN3-Lewis acid and Lewis base citrate [30–32]. As a result, the guanidinium cation presented in TAG-CDs provide the stronger electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding than guanidine is due to the presence of CN6 and protons, respectively. The Lewis acidic quality of the positive center carbon atom in TAG-CDs allow to polarize the associates, which enhances its fundamental reactivity. Therefore, the Lewis acid TAG-CDs can interact with its ionic pair citrate-Lewis base through electrostatic force and hydrogen bonding strongly [30–32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism, quenching and binding constants
ce:section.ce:para:
The obtained quenching sequence of TAG-CDs with citrate was studied through Stern Volmer plot as shown in Fig. S4A. The non-linear fit was obtained for Stern-Volmer equation as shown below
F 0
/ F
=
1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]
where “F0 ” and “F” are the fluorescence emission intensities of TAG-CDs in absence and presence of each concentrations of citrate, respectively. “Q” is the concentration of quencher citrate and “Ksv ” is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The obtained non linearity Stern Volmer plot indicates that both static and dynamic quenching process taking place in the system of TAG-CDs interaction with citrate. From that plot the quenching constant was found to be 2.99 × 105 L mol−1. Further the binding constant was evaluated by using the bellowed equation
log
(
F 0
- F /
F 0
)
=
log K
A
+
n log
[ Q ]
where “F0 ” and “F” are the fluorescence emission intensities of TAG-CDs in absence and presence of each concentrations of citrate, respectively. “Q” is the concentration of quencher citrate and “KA ” is the binding constant. Based on the obtained intercept value from the plot Fig. S4B of log (F0-F/F0) versus log [Q], the binding constant was calculated to be 5.1 × 104 L mol−1. The obtained quenching and binding constant values indicate that, the probe TAG-CDs having good interaction ability with citrate molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of TAG-CDs towards detection of citrate
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of citrate could be affected by the reaction time, pH and other interferences. Hence the sensing conditions were carefully examined in order to obtain the best detection results. To explore whether the present probe TAG-CDs produce selective response towards citrate over some commonly present carboxylate biomolecules. For this, the probe TAG-CDs were treated with 0.1 µM of each analytes citrate, benzoate, succinate, oxalate, acetate, sulfite, fumarate, glutamate, phosphate, malonate, nitrate, fluoride, lead, mercury and cadmium separately and measured the fluorescence emission behavior. Except for citrate treated TAG-CDs solution, no significant fluorescence intensity changes were observed with all other solutions (Fig. 4 ). After interacting the probe TAG-CDs with citrate via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction, the emitting nature of the TAG-CDs was quenched well. Additionally, the interference tolerance ability of the present probe was checked with the 0.05 mM of above interferences along with 0.1 µM of citrate. The results were depicted in (Fig. 4), it indicates that the citrate detection was not disturbed by 500–fold excess of possible interferences. Hence, the citrate detection in biological system would not interfere with the other possible analytes.

Further, a fluorescent probe for bioimaging applications require an unfluctuating fluorescence signals in biological environment mainly in the relay on range of pH. For that the pH titration was evaluated to explore whether the less acidic environment (which is suitable for cell lysosomes and mitochondria) and neutral pH conditions could disturb the emission signal read outs. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the emission intensity changes of bare TAT-CDs at different pH was shown stable signals over the pH ranges of 4 to 8 and the emission intensity affected at in basic conditions but it is not meet the anion’s quenching level. After introducing 0.6 µM of citrate to the each pH solutions, the quenched emission signals were observed. However, the stable and highest emission intensity was observed at pH 7 as well as the quenching efficiency was high at pH 7, which makes the present probe TAG-CDs will be suitable for fluorescence imaging in live cells. Again, the probe TAG-CDs hold the pH of 7, the major advantage is we could use as such the synthesis one and no need of pretreatment with alkaline solution to adjust the pH to get good emitting nature. Some CDs are emitting only in the basic pH media, which has the drawbacks such as uncomfortable to the biological systems. Further, the reaction time taking to produce a stable response while reacting with citrate was analyzed with different concentrations (50, 200, 400, 600 and 800 nM) of citrate. The emission response was monitored for different concentration of citrate added probe solutions at different time intervals. The plot was drawn between observed emission intensity at each concentrations of citrate reacted with TAG-CDs versus the reaction time was shown in Fig. 5B. As seen from Fig. 5B, initially the quenching efficiency increases i.e., emission intensity decreases rapidly and stays stable after 15 min of reaction time, which expressing the optimal reaction time is of 15 min. Hence all experiments were carried out with 15 mins of incubation/reaction time. Above all experiments clearly states that the present probe TAG-CDs show selective towards citrate among the 500 – fold excess of other fool of interferences, fast response in 15 mins of reaction time and mainly detect citrate in biological conditions such as good emitting nature in physiological pH, which allows TAG-CDs to become more suitable fluorescent bioimaging reagent for tracing intracellular citrate in live cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and bioimaging ability of TAG-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The good optical and sensing performance of probe TAG-CDs prompted us to explore their practical utilities for citrate detection in live cells. Since the detection limit (4 nM) for citrate using the TAG-CDs, which is well bellowed the concentration of citrate in human body (50–200 mM, 2–20 mM for healthy and cancerous persons) encouraged us to do further applications. Initially the cytotoxicity effect of TAG-CDs was evaluated in MCF7 (breast cancer cells), Lewis (lung cancer cells) and MRC-9 (healthy lung tissue cells) with conventional MTT assay with different concentrations of probe (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 7 and 10 mg of TAG-CDs were dissolved in 10 ml of DI water). The cell survival ratio obtained from the MTT assay analysis reveals that the present probe TAG-CDs had low cytotoxicity effects, more than 80 % of the cells are in live even at 10 mg/ml of probe solution in all the cell lines (Fig. S5). Therefore, the probe TAG-CDs could be utilized as a good bioimaging reagent. The visualization of citrate in live cells was explored using living MCF-7 cells. The live cells stained with 0.2 mg/ml solution of TAG-CDs for 30 mins after the excess probe solution was removed by rinsing with medium. The probe stained cells were again treated with 1 µM of citrate about 15 mins, then rinsed with cell culture medium. The live cell images of both the probe stained and citrate treated cells were recorded by using confocal microscopy, the obtained images were displayed in Fig. 6 . As seen from Fig. 6a, the probe stained cells were expressed bright blue fluorescence. After the probe stained cells treated with citrate showed less intensity or weak blue fluorescence. Interestingly, the TAG-CDs located predominantly in cell nuclei, which is due to the positively charged small size TAG-CDs easily penetrate the cell membrane, cytoplasm and reach the nuclei [42–45] and the nucleus targeting ability of TAG-CDs was tested against Lewis cell lines (Fig. S6). The obtained microscopy images showed that the probe TAG-CDs has low cytotoxicity, easily permeated into live cells and performed with contrast images in presence of citrate, allowing TAG-CDs to be a good fluorescent bioimaging agent for citrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of citrate in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the ability of present probe for its practical utility citrate was determined from urine samples (the samples were collected from our research team members) and out coming results were validated with HPLC method. For this, three urine samples were collected, the 1 ml of urine sample was diluted by ten times and then added with TAG-CDs. The emission intensity quenching observed in TAG-CDs with citrate was compared with real sample added emission intensity based on the comparative method. Further, a known concentration of citrate introduced and evaluate the citrate level and validated with HPLC method. The obtained results are given in Table 2 . The obtained citrate value from the present proposed method fairly matched with HPLC validation value.

In summary, a new TAG based CDs were synthesized from its precursor TAG through single step hydrothermal method. In nature the precursor TAG has good biocompatibility and selective towards Y-shaped trianions such as citrate. The prepared TAG-CDs were successfully utilized for the selective detection of citrate. The sensing mechanistic strategy involves Lewis acid mechanism, the electrostatic interaction between the Lewis acid carbocation in Y-aromaticity (CN6) guanidinium moiety and citrate. Also, the guanidine protons interacted with citrate via hydrogen bonding interaction too. During binding with citrate, the emission intensity of TAG-CDs at 396 nm was quenched linearly with each addition of citrate, based on the linearity curve the detection limit was found to be 4 nM (S/N = 3). The probe TAG-CDs shown good selectivity towards citrate, high interference tolerance up to 500 – fold, stable response in 15 mins of reaction time, good response in biological pH conditions. The major advantages of the present study is the highly emitting TAG-CDs with neutral pH were prepared from its non-emitting precursors. Some CDs are emitting in basic conditions, which are not well suitable for usage in bioimaging reagents. Since the precursor, TAG itself has good biocompatible, the probe TAG-CDs having good cytotoxicity effects even more than 80 % of the cells are live at high concentration of probe (10 mg/ml). For the first time we prepared CDs from TAG and successfully applied for the imaging of citrate at intracellular level in live MCF-7 cells. The probe easily penetrates in to cell membrane and delivered a contrast intracellular images in absence (high intensity blue emission) and presence (weak blue emission) of citrate.

Kanagaraj Rajalakshmi: Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Tongtong Deng: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization. Selvaraj Muthusamy: Methodology, Data curation. Meng Xie: Supervision. Jimin Xie: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Kang-Bong Lee: Data curation. Yuanguo Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Data curation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Grant (No. 2019M661757) and Jiangsu postdoctoral research funding program (No. 2021K102B).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120622.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
